content: The angle which the line $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{-1}=\frac{z}{0}$ makes with the positive direction of Y -axis is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given line is }\\ &\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{-1}=\frac{z}{0} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore$ Direction ratios of line are: $1,-1,0$

We know that direction ratio of $y$-axis are $0,1,0$

$$\begin{aligned} \cos \theta & =\frac{\vec{b}_1 \cdot \vec{b}_2}{\left|\vec{b}_1\right| \cdot\left|\vec{b}_2\right|} \\ & =\frac{(\hat{i}-\hat{j}) \cdot(\hat{j})}{\sqrt{(1)^2+(-1)^2} \cdot \sqrt{(1)^2}} \\ & {\left[\because \overrightarrow{b_1}=\hat{i}-\hat{j} \text { and } \overrightarrow{b_2}=\hat{j}\right] } \\ & =\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad \cos \theta=\cos \left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)$

$\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{3 \pi}{4}$

content: The Cartesian equation of the line passing through the point $(1,-3,2)$ and parallel to the line $\vec{r}=(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+\lambda \hat{j}+(2 \lambda-1) \hat{k}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given line is }\\ &\begin{aligned} \vec{r} & =(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+\lambda \hat{j}+(2 \lambda-1) \hat{k} \\ \vec{r} & =(2 \hat{i}-\hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

which is of the form

$$\vec{r}=a+\lambda \vec{b}$$

$\therefore$ Required line is

$$\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y+3}{1}=\frac{z-2}{2}$$

[As lines passes through $(1,-3,2)$ and having Direction cosines $(1,1,2)]$

content: If a line makes angles of $$90^{\circ}, 135^{\circ}$$ and $$45^{\circ}$$ with the $$x, y$$ and $$z$$ axes respectively, then its direction cosines are:
The angle between the lines $$2 x=3 y=-z$$ and $$6 x=-y=-4 z$$ is:
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{rlrl} 2 x =3 y=-z && 6 x =-y=-4 z \\ \frac{x}{3} =\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{-6} && \frac{x}{2} =\frac{y}{-12}=\frac{z}{-3} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \cos \theta & =\left|\frac{a_1 a_2+b_1 b_2+c_1 c_2}{\sqrt{a_1^2+b_1^2+c_1^2} \sqrt{a_2^2+b_2^2+c_2^2}}\right| \\ \cos \theta & =\left|\frac{3 \times 2+2(-12)+(-6)(-3)}{\sqrt{3^2+2^2+6^2} \sqrt{2^2+(-12)^2+(-3)^2}}\right| \\ \cos \theta & =\left|\frac{6-24+18}{7 \cdot \sqrt{157}}\right|=0 \\ \therefore \quad \theta & =\frac{\pi}{2}=90^{\circ} \end{aligned}$$

content: Find the vector equation of the line passing through the point $(2,3,-5)$ and making equal angles with the co-ordinate axes.
explanation:
Given $$\alpha=\beta=\gamma$$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} & \cos ^2 \alpha+\cos ^2 \beta+\cos ^2 \beta=1 \\ & \therefore \quad 3 \cos ^2 \alpha=1\quad [\because \alpha=\beta=\gamma] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { or, }\quad\cos ^2 \alpha & =\frac{1}{3} \\ \text { or, }\quad\cos \alpha & = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, $\cos \alpha=\cos \beta=\cos \gamma= \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=l=m=n$

Let required equation of line is $\vec{r}=\vec{a}+k \vec{b}$

Here,

$$\begin{aligned} \vec{a} & =2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k} \\ \vec{b} & = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \end{aligned}$$

Thus, required equation is

$$\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}) \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})$$

content:
(a) Find the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point $(2,3,-8)$ to the line $\frac{4-x}{2}=\frac{y}{6}=\frac{1-z}{3}$.

OR

(b). Find the shortest distance between the lines $\mathrm{L}_1 \& \mathrm{~L}_2$ given below: $\mathrm{L}_1$ : The line passing through $(2,-1,1)$ and parallel to $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{z}{3} \mathrm{~L}_2: \vec{r}=\hat{i}+(2 \mu+1) \hat{j}-(\mu+2) \hat{k}$.
explanation:
(a) Given line is $\frac{4-x}{2}=\frac{y}{6}=\frac{1-z}{3}$

or, $\quad \frac{x-4}{-2}=\frac{y}{6}=\frac{z-1}{-3}=\lambda$

Now, the co-ordinates of any points $Q$ on the line are $(-2 \lambda+4,6 \lambda,-3 \lambda+1)$.

Also, the given point is $p(2,3,-8)$

The direction ratios of PQ are $-2 \lambda+4-2,6 \lambda-3,-3 \lambda$

$$+1+8 \text { i.e., }-2 \lambda+2,6 \lambda-3,-3 \lambda+9$$

Also, the direction cosines of the given line are $-2,6-3$.

If $\mathrm{PQ} \perp$ line, then

$$\begin{aligned} -2(-2 \lambda+2)+6(6 \lambda-3)-3(-3 \lambda+9) & =0 \\ 4 \lambda-4+36 \lambda-18+9 \lambda-27 & =0 \\ 49 \lambda-49 & =0 \\ \lambda & =1 \end{aligned}$$

Now, the foot of the perpendicular is

$$[-2(1)+4,6(1),-3(1)+1] \text { i.e., }(2,6,-2)$$

Hence, the distance PQ is

$$\begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{(2-2)^2+(3-6)^2+(-8+2)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{0+9+36}=\sqrt{45}=3 \sqrt{5} \text { units } \end{aligned}$$

OR

(b) Given, $\mathrm{L}_1$ : The line passing through $(2,-1,1)$ and parallel to $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{z}{3}\quad\text{.... (i)}$

Direction ratios of line (i) are $(1,1,3)$

$\therefore$ Vector equation of line passing through $(2,-1,1)$ having direction ratios is

$\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})\quad\text{.... (i)}$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Given, } & & \mathrm{L}_2: \vec{r} & =\hat{i}+(3 \mu+1) \hat{j}-(\mu+2) \hat{k} \\ \text { or, } & & \vec{r} & =(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k})+\mu(3 \hat{j}-\hat{k})\quad\text{.... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Now, shortest distance between lines (ii) \& (iii) is given by

Here, $\quad \overrightarrow{a_1}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \overrightarrow{a_2}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k})$

$$d=\frac{\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right) \cdot\left(\vec{a}_2 \times \vec{a}_1\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|}$$

$$\overrightarrow{b_1}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}), \overrightarrow{b_2}=(3 \hat{j}-\hat{k})$$

Now,

$$\begin{array}{r} \overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}=(1-2) \hat{i}+(1+1) \hat{j}+(-2-1) \hat{k} \\ =-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{b_1} \times \overrightarrow{b_2} & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 3 & -1 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\hat{i}(-1-9)-\hat{j}(-1-0)+\hat{k}(3-0) \\ & =-10 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { and } \quad \left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right| & =\sqrt{(-10)^2+(1)^2+(3)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{100+1+9} \\ & =\sqrt{110} \\ \therefore \quad d & =\left|\frac{(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})(-10 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{110}}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{10+2-3}{\sqrt{110}}\right|=\frac{9}{\sqrt{110}} \text { unit } \end{aligned}$$

content: Find the vector and the cartesian equations of a line that passes through the point $$A(1,2,-1)$$ and parallel to the line $$5 x-25=14-7 y=35 z$$.
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given point } A(1,2,-1) \\ & \text { Given line } 5 x-25=14-7 y,=35 z, \\ & =5(x-5),=-7(y-2),=35 z, \\ & =\frac{x-5}{7}=\frac{y-7}{-5},=\frac{z}{1} \text { Divide by } 35 \end{aligned}$$

Direction ration of the line $$(7,-5,1)$$

$$\therefore$$ Direction ratio of the parallel line $$(7,-5,1)$$

Equation of the line passing through the point $$\mathrm{A} (1,2,-1)$$ and parallel to the given line

$$\frac{x-1}{7}=\frac{y-2}{-5}=\frac{z+1}{1}$$

vector form of the line

$$\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}+\lambda(7 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+\hat{k})$$

content:
(a) Find the coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point $$P(0,2,3)$$ to the line $$\frac{x+3}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z+4}{3}$$.

OR

(b) Three vectors $$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}$$ and $$\vec{c}$$ satisfy the condition $$\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0}$$. Evaluate the quantity $$\mu= \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}$$, if $$|\vec{a}|=3,|\vec{b}|=4$$ and $$|\vec{c}|=2$$
explanation:
(a) P(0, 2, 3)

$$\text { line } \frac{x+3}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z+4}{3}$$

General point on the line is

$$[(5 \lambda-3),(2 \lambda+1),(3 \lambda-4)]$$

Direction ratio of the perpendicular line

$$\begin{gathered} {[(5 \lambda-3),(2 \lambda-1),(3 \lambda-7)]} \\ \therefore 5(5 \lambda-3)+2(2 \lambda-1)+3(3 \lambda-7)=0 \\ 25 \lambda-15+4 \lambda-2+9 \lambda-21=0 \\ 38 \lambda-38=1 \\ \lambda=1 \end{gathered}$$

$$\therefore$$ foot of perpendicular line is

$$\begin{aligned} & {[(5-3),(2+1),(3-4)} \\ & (2,3,-1) \end{aligned}$$

OR

(b)

$$\begin{gathered} \vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0} \\ (\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}) \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c})=0 \\ \vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{b} \\ +\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{c}=0 \\ |\vec{a}|^2+|\vec{b}|^2+|\vec{c}|^2+2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a})=0 \\ 3^2+4^2+2^2+2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a})=0 \\ 2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a})=-29 \\ \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}=-\frac{29}{2} \end{gathered}$$
